key_points: The political landscape post-7th century was characterized by 'Fractured Sovereignty,' where Samantas transitioned into 'Maha-samantas' or 'Maha-mandaleshvaras' by mobilizing local resources and military autonomy.

The Chola economy was 'Temple-Centric'; temples were not merely religious centers but nuclei of settlements, hubs of craft production (bronze-casting), and significant landholders managing specialized labor.

The 'Tripartite Struggle' for Kanauj illustrates the geopolitical significance of the Indo-Gangetic plain as a symbol of 'Chakravartin' (universal) authority among the Gurjara-Pratihara, Rashtrakuta, and Pala dynasties.
Chola local self-government was a sophisticated democratic model where the 'Sabha' (assembly of Brahmana landholders) operated through specialized committees for irrigation, gardens, and temples.

Prashastis serve as a 'Source of Contradiction'; while they offer genealogical data, historians like Kalhana (author of Rajatarangini) criticized traditional panegyrics and used critical evidence to record the history of Kashmir.
examples: Uttaramerur Inscriptions: Detailed records in Chingleput (Tamil Nadu) explaining the strict eligibility criteria (age, property, Vedic knowledge) for 'Sabha' membership.
Kudavolai System: A unique 'lottery' method where names of candidates were written on palm leaves and drawn from an earthenware pot by a young boy.
Rajendra I’s Naval Expedition: The expansion of Chola influence into Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia to secure maritime trade routes and demonstrate blue-water naval capabilities.
Brahmadeya Management: The process where each land grant to Brahmanas was recorded in detail on copper plates to ensure long-term tax-free status and legal protection.
formulas: Imperial Legitimacy = $\text{Patronage of Brahmanas} + \text{Monumental Architecture (Temples)} + \text{Military Conquests}$
Chola Local Governance = $\text{Ur (Peasant Assembly)} + \text{Sabha (Brahmana Assembly)} + \text{Nagaram (Trader Organizations)}$
exam_tips: Analyze the role of the 'Nadu' as an administrative unit that performed judicial functions and collected taxes under the supervision of the central Chola government.
Understand that the 'Vetti' tax (forced labor) highlights the high level of state control over the physical labor of the peasantry.
Critically evaluate why Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni’s raids were specifically targeted at temples—they were repositories of immense social and economic wealth.
Remember that 'Araiyar' and 'Muventavelan' were high-sounding titles given to rich landowners to integrate them into the royal administration.
